risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]." Comment: This describes a computerized urn randomization procedure, which is a proper random allocation method; the additional note that groups were balanced on baseline characteristics is consistent with a controlled random process.

_evidence.method_quote: Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. The two conditions were balanced on gender, age, ethnic background, pain level recorded at baseline, and RDC axis I diagnoses.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were assigned using a computerized urn randomization procedure with balance on several baseline characteristics.
context_clues: balanced on gender, age, ethnic background, pain level recorded at baseline, and RDC axis I diagnoses

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. ... The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments." Comment: This describes a centralized assignment process handled by the Project Coordinator at intake, which means the recruiter could not foresee the upcoming allocation before enrollment. Although the text does not use the exact phrase 'central allocation,' the described procedure indicates the assignment was generated/entered centrally rather than from an खुले schedule visible to recruiters, so allocation concealment is adequately protected.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments.
_evidence.note: Sequence generation is described as 'a computerized urn randomization procedure', but no separate concealment detail beyond assignment by the Project Coordinator is provided.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The therapist took care to not deliver any kind of cognitive-behavioral treatment to these patients." Comment: This indicates personnel were aware of treatment assignment rather than blinded. The study outcomes were patient-reported subjective measures ("Characteristic Pain", "Depressive Symptoms", "Pain-related Interference"), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence both participants' responses and the therapists' delivery of the intervention. The article also notes that the assessor was "not blinded to treatment condition," reinforcing that blinding was not maintained overall, although that is more directly relevant to detection bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote: All treatment sessions in both conditions were audiotaped and reviewed for adherence to the treatment protocol by the first author. ... The therapist took care to not deliver any kind of cognitive-behavioral treatment to these patients. ... A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly unblinded. The study’s main outcomes were patient-reported pain, depressive symptoms, and pain-related interference (e.g., Characteristic Pain, CES-D, MPI Interference), which are subjective and could be influenced by knowledge of allocation; therefore the domain is High risk under the worst-outcome rule.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions." Comment: The paper reports overall follow-up rates and says losses were equivalent, and it states an ITT/mixed-model approach with maximum likelihood estimation, but it does not provide arm-specific analysed numbers or loss counts for the outcome time points. The extracted evidence notes that attrition is reported only overall rather than per arm, so the available information does not let us verify whether loss was low, balanced, or outcome-related for the analysed outcomes.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52)” and “At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions.”

“Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52)” and “At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: intent-to-treat; mixed model regression procedure; maximum likelihood estimation
_evidence.notes: Attrition information is reported for overall follow-up (“88% … at posttreatment” and “73% … at 52 weeks”) rather than per arm; no arm-specific analysed numbers or loss counts were provided.
